votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if the boolean b matches whether the length of the string is a prime number.
The Python code correctly implements primality testing and checks if the length of the string is prime, matching the Coq specification.
The Python implementation of is_prime correctly identifies prime numbers, and the spec matches the Coq definition.

The Coq spec says b=true iff length(s) is prime (using Coq's prime predicate on Z). The Python code returns b == is_prime(len(s)), where is_prime correctly implements primality testing (returns False for n<2, checks divisibility up to sqrt(n)). Both agree on concrete inputs: b should be True exactly when the string length is a prime number.

The Python code checks if b equals is_prime(len(s)), where is_prime correctly implements primality testing (returns False for n<2, checks divisibility up to sqrt(n)). Both agree on concrete inputs: b should be True exactly when the string length is prime.

Both require b to match whether the string length is prime; is_prime correctly handles n>=0 and the precondition is trivial True.
Both specify that b is true exactly when the string length is prime; the Python primality test on len(s) matches the Coq prime over Z.of_nat(length s) for all concrete inputs.

Precondition always true; spec states b is true iff length(s) is prime. Python checks b == is_prime(len(s)), and is_prime correctly identifies primes for all nonnegative lengths, matching Coq’s prime on Z.of_nat(length).
